naturalLanguage: A Salish blanket woven on a two-bar loom from mountain goat wool and woolly dog wool is an artifact — a human-made textile object. SUMO classifies it by material composition (fiber) and manufacturing process (weaving).
Weaving a Salish blanket on a two-bar loom is a manufacturing process — setting the warp at a defined sett, interlacing weft threads, managing tension, and building the cloth from the bottom up.
Salish blanket weaving is also a heritage skill — encoding knowledge of fiber preparation, dye plants, design composition, and the two-bar loom system passed within Coast Salish weaving families.
A cedar root basket is an artifact — a human-made container woven from prepared cedar root using coiling, plaiting, or twining techniques depending on the nation.

Preparing cedar roots for basketry — harvesting from young cedars, splitting, scraping, sizing — is traditional material culture encoding knowledge of which roots to harvest, when to harvest, and how to process for different basket types.
A Coast Salish two-bar loom is an artifact — a weaving tool consisting of two horizontal bars held apart by tension, with warp threads hanging vertically between them.

Coast Salish formline design is a cultural practice — a systematic visual design grammar distinct from northern NW Coast formline, expressed primarily on spindle whorls, house posts, and ceremonial objects.

A Cowichan sweater is an artifact — a dense, water-resistant knitted garment produced from minimally processed Salish wool using knitting needles (post-contact tool) and pre-contact design traditions.

Cowichan knitting is a heritage skill — a post-contact adaptation that integrated European knitting technique with Coast Salish fiber knowledge and design tradition, producing a new textile form specific to Quw'utsun' (Cowichan) people.
